title: Health & Emergencies
shortSummary: Explain how you feel, ask for help, and handle a medical or emergency situation in Turkish.
culturalNote: Health is a frequent and caring topic in Turkish conversation, and wishing someone geçmiş olsun (may it pass / get well soon) is a heartfelt phrase you will hear constantly, even from strangers and shopkeepers when they learn you are unwell. Turkey has a wide network of pharmacies (eczane), marked by a red E, where pharmacists give practical advice and many medicines without a prescription; outside hours, a nöbetçi eczane (on-duty pharmacy) stays open. The universal emergency number is 112, which covers ambulance, fire, and police. State and private hospitals (hastane) serve large cities well, and many doctors in private clinics speak some English. When describing pain, Turks use the structure '... ağrıyor' (my ... hurts), as in başım ağrıyor (my head hurts). A warm geçmiş olsun in reply to anyone's illness is always appreciated.

keyPhrases.english: I need to see a doctor

grammar.rule: Describing pain with body part plus ağrıyor
grammar.explanation: To say a body part hurts, add a possessive ending to it and follow with ağrıyor (it hurts): başım ağrıyor (my head hurts), midem ağrıyor (my stomach hurts), boğazım ağrıyor (my throat hurts). The body part takes the -im 'my' ending and the verb stays singular.
grammar.commonMistake: Forgetting the possessive ending. It is başım ağrıyor (my head), not baş ağrıyor for 'my head hurts'.

grammar.explanation: To say you must or should do something, add -meli or -malı (with vowel harmony) plus a personal ending to the verb stem: gitmeliyim (I must go), dinlenmeliyim (I should rest). This expresses obligation or strong advice.

examples.en: I must go to the doctor.

grammar.commonMistake: Using the wrong vowel. After a front vowel it is gitmeliyim; after a back vowel it is almalıyım.

dialogue.setup: A traveller feels unwell and goes to a pharmacy for advice.

lines.speaker: Müşteri
lines.english: Hello, I'm sick. My head hurts a lot.

lines.speaker: Eczacı
lines.english: Get well soon. Do you have a fever?

lines.speaker: Müşteri
lines.english: A little. I need some medicine.

lines.speaker: Eczacı
lines.native: Bu ağrı kesiciyi alın ve dinlenin.

lines.speaker: Müşteri
lines.english: Thank you. If it doesn't pass, I must see a doctor.

quiz.question: How do you say 'Help!' in Turkish?
quiz.explanation: Yardım edin! is the polite/plural call for help.

quiz.question: Which phrase means 'Get well soon'?
quiz.explanation: Geçmiş olsun means 'may it pass', said to someone who is unwell.

quiz.question: Baş_____ ağrıyor. (My head hurts)
quiz.explanation: The possessive -ım on baş gives başım, 'my head'.

quiz.question: How do you say 'I need to see a doctor'?
quiz.explanation: Gitmeliyim means 'I must go'; doktora is 'to the doctor'.
